era_context: The year 1791 falls in the heart of the Age of Revolutions. The French Revolution, now in its third year, was radicalizing as the monarchy attempted to reassert control and failed. Across the Atlantic, the newly independent United States was consolidating its constitutional order with the Bill of Rights, while in the Caribbean, the Haitian Revolution erupted — the largest and most successful slave revolt in history. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor (who had abdicated in favor of Jiaqing in 1796, but this is 1791 so Qianlong still reigns) was at the tail end of its 'High Qing' prosperity, the Ottoman Empire was fighting a losing war against Russia, and European colonial powers continued to expand in India, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Pacific Island societies beyond early European contact
Interior Southeast Asian kingdoms beyond Burmese and Siamese court records

events.title: Flight to Varennes
events.description: On 20–21 June 1791, King Louis XVI and the royal family attempted to flee Paris in disguise, aiming to reach the royalist fortress at Montmédy near the Austrian border. They were recognized and arrested at Varennes-en-Argonne. The failed escape devastated whatever remained of popular trust in the monarchy, radicalized republican sentiment, and made the fiction of a king cooperating willingly with the Revolution untenable. It accelerated the trajectory toward the abolition of the monarchy and the king's eventual trial and execution.
key_figures: Louis XVI (King of France)
Marie Antoinette (Queen of France)
Axel von Fersen (Swedish nobleman, organized the escape)
Jean-Baptiste Drouet (postmaster who recognized the king)

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts (e.g., L'Ami du peuple, Révolutions de Paris)
Proceedings of the National Assembly, June 1791
Timothy Tackett, When the King Took Flight (2003)

events.certainty_note: One of the most thoroughly documented episodes of the French Revolution, with abundant contemporary sources from multiple perspectives.

events.title: Champ de Mars massacre
events.description: On 17 July 1791, a crowd gathered at the Champ de Mars to sign a petition demanding the king's abdication following the Flight to Varennes. The National Guard under the Marquis de Lafayette opened fire on the demonstrators, killing dozens (estimates range from 12 to 50). The massacre marked a decisive split between moderate constitutional monarchists and radical republicans, drove the Cordeliers and Jacobin clubs underground temporarily, and foreshadowed the more radical phases of the Revolution.
key_figures: Marquis de Lafayette (commander of the National Guard)
Jean Sylvain Bailly (Mayor of Paris, who declared martial law)
Georges Danton (radical leader, fled Paris after the event)

sources.name: Procès-verbaux of the Paris Commune and National Assembly debates
Contemporary pamphlets and newspapers
David Andress, The Terror: Civil War in the French Revolution (2005)

events.certainty_note: Well documented, though casualty figures vary across sources.

events.title: French Constitution of 1791 adopted
events.description: On 3 September 1791, the National Constituent Assembly completed France's first written constitution, establishing a constitutional monarchy with a unicameral Legislative Assembly. The king accepted it on 14 September. The constitution enshrined civil liberties from the Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789), but restricted voting to 'active citizens' — men who paid a minimum level of taxation — excluding women, the poor, and servants. It was effectively dead on arrival: the Flight to Varennes had destroyed trust in the king, and the constitution would be superseded within a year by the declaration of a republic.
key_figures: Louis XVI (King of France, who accepted the constitution)
Emmanuel Joseph Sieyès (constitutional theorist)
Honoré Gabriel Riqueti, comte de Mirabeau (influential deputy, died April 1791)

sources.name: Text of the Constitution of 1791 (Archives parlementaires)
Keith Michael Baker, Inventing the French Revolution (1990)

events.certainty_note: The constitutional text itself survives; its adoption and context are extensively documented.

events.title: Olympe de Gouges publishes Declaration of the Rights of Woman
events.description: In September 1791, playwright and political activist Olympe de Gouges published the Déclaration des droits de la femme et de la citoyenne (Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen), modeled on the 1789 Declaration of the Rights of Man. It argued that women were born free and equal to men, demanded female suffrage, and challenged the exclusion of women from citizenship rights in the new constitution. The text was largely ignored in its time but is now regarded as a foundational document in the history of feminism and human rights.
key_figures: Olympe de Gouges (author and activist)

sources.name: Olympe de Gouges, Déclaration des droits de la femme et de la citoyenne (1791), original pamphlet
Joan Wallach Scott, Only Paradoxes to Offer: French Feminists and the Rights of Man (1996)

events.certainty_note: The text survives in its original published form.

events.title: Haitian Revolution begins (Bois Caïman ceremony and August uprising)
events.description: On the night of 14 August 1791, enslaved people gathered at Bois Caïman in the northern plain of Saint-Domingue for a Vodou ceremony that is traditionally regarded as the catalyst of the Haitian Revolution. On 22–23 August, a massive coordinated slave revolt erupted across the northern province, destroying sugar plantations and killing hundreds of enslavers. Within weeks, tens of thousands of enslaved people were in open rebellion under leaders including Boukman Dutty. This was the beginning of the only successful large-scale slave revolution in history, which would eventually lead to Haitian independence in 1804 and profoundly shake the Atlantic slave system.
key_figures: Boukman Dutty (early leader of the revolt, killed in November 1791)
Cécile Fatiman (Vodou priestess, traditionally associated with Bois Caïman)
Dutty Boukman's unnamed co-conspirators among enslaved plantation workers

sources.name: Antoine Dalmas, Histoire de la révolution de Saint-Domingue (1814) — earliest detailed account of the ceremony, written by a hostile white colonist
Colonial administrative correspondence and planter reports, August–September 1791 (Archives nationales d'outre-mer)
Laurent Dubois, Avengers of the New World: The Story of the Haitian Revolution (2004)

events.certainty_note: The August 1791 uprising is abundantly documented in colonial records. The Bois Caïman ceremony is traditional; its historicity is debated by scholars (see disconfirming evidence section), though the revolt itself is beyond question.

events.title: United States Bill of Rights ratified
events.description: On 15 December 1791, the first ten amendments to the United States Constitution — collectively known as the Bill of Rights — were ratified after Virginia became the eleventh state to approve them. The amendments guaranteed fundamental civil liberties including freedom of speech, religion, and the press; the right to bear arms; protections against unreasonable search and seizure; and due process rights. Originally proposed by James Madison in 1789 to address Anti-Federalist concerns about centralized power, these amendments became the bedrock of American constitutional law and jurisprudence.
key_figures: James Madison (principal author)
George Washington (President of the United States)
Thomas Jefferson (Secretary of State, advocate for a bill of rights)

sources.name: Original engrossed Bill of Rights manuscript (National Archives)
Congressional records and ratification documents of state legislatures
Akhil Reed Amar, The Bill of Rights: Creation and Reconstruction (1998)

events.certainty_note: Original documents survive in the National Archives; ratification dates are documented in state legislative records.

events.title: Canada Act (Constitutional Act) divides Quebec
events.description: On 10 June 1791, the British Parliament passed the Constitutional Act, which divided the Province of Quebec into Upper Canada (predominantly English-speaking, roughly modern Ontario) and Lower Canada (predominantly French-speaking, roughly modern Quebec). Each province received its own elected legislative assembly and appointed legislative council. The act was intended to manage tensions between Anglophone Loyalist settlers and the Francophone majority, but it created structural political conflicts — particularly in Lower Canada — that would fester for decades and contribute to the Rebellions of 1837–38.
key_figures: William Pitt the Younger (British Prime Minister)
Guy Carleton, 1st Baron Dorchester (Governor of Quebec)

sources.name: Constitutional Act, 1791 (31 Geo. III, c. 31), Parliamentary records
Parliamentary debates (Hansard)
J.M. Bumsted, The Peoples of Canada: A Pre-Confederation History (2003)

events.certainty_note: Statutory text and parliamentary records survive.

events.title: Treaty of Sistova ends Austro-Turkish War
events.region: Ottoman Empire / Habsburg Empire (Sistova, modern Svishtov, Bulgaria)
events.description: On 4 August 1791, the Habsburg Empire and the Ottoman Empire signed the Treaty of Sistova, ending the Austro-Turkish War that had begun in 1788. Austria returned most of its conquests, including Belgrade, to the Ottomans, gaining only the town of Orsova and minor border adjustments. The treaty reflected Austria's need to redirect military resources toward the growing crisis with revolutionary France and the Polish situation. It marked the end of Habsburg expansionism at Ottoman expense in the Balkans for the time being.
key_figures: Leopold II (Holy Roman Emperor)
Selim III (Ottoman Sultan)

sources.name: Treaty text in diplomatic archives (Haus-, Hof- und Staatsarchiv, Vienna)
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870: An Empire Besieged (2007)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and diplomatic correspondence survive in both Austrian and Ottoman archives.

events.title: Declaration of Pillnitz
events.description: On 27 August 1791, Emperor Leopold II and King Frederick William II of Prussia issued the Declaration of Pillnitz, stating that the situation of the French king was a matter of concern to all European sovereigns and hinting at military intervention to restore Louis XVI's authority. The declaration was deliberately vague — Leopold intended it as a bluff contingent on British support that he knew would not come — but it was read in France as a direct threat and inflamed revolutionary hostility toward foreign monarchies, contributing to the march toward war in 1792.
key_figures: Leopold II (Holy Roman Emperor)
Frederick William II (King of Prussia)
Louis Auguste Le Tonnelier de Breteuil (royalist agent)

sources.name: Text of the Declaration of Pillnitz (diplomatic correspondence)
T.C.W. Blanning, The French Revolutionary Wars 1787–1802 (1996)

events.certainty_note: The declaration text survives and its context is well documented in diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Mozart dies in Vienna; completes The Magic Flute and Requiem left unfinished
events.description: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart died on 5 December 1791 in Vienna at age 35 from a still-debated illness. In his final year he composed some of his greatest works: The Magic Flute (Die Zauberflöte), premiered on 30 September 1791 at the Theater auf der Wieden; La clemenza di Tito, composed for Leopold II's coronation as King of Bohemia; the Clarinet Concerto in A major; and the unfinished Requiem in D minor, later completed by his student Franz Xaver Süssmayr. His death left an enormous void in European musical life and generated enduring myths about poisoning and pauper's burial.
key_figures: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (composer)
Emanuel Schikaneder (librettist and impresario, The Magic Flute)
Franz Xaver Süssmayr (student, completed the Requiem)
Constanze Mozart (wife)

sources.name: Mozart's personal correspondence and family letters (Mozart: Briefe und Aufzeichnungen, ed. Bauer and Deutsch)
Viennese death records and parish registers
Maynard Solomon, Mozart: A Life (1995)

events.certainty_note: Mozart's death date and final works are thoroughly documented. Cause of death remains medically debated.

events.title: Suppression of the Avignon Papacy and annexation to France
events.description: On 14 September 1791, the French National Assembly voted to annex the papal territories of Avignon and the Comtat Venaissin, which had been papal possessions since the 14th century. This followed a local insurrection and plebiscite in which inhabitants voted to join France. The annexation was one of several revolutionary challenges to papal authority and contributed to the deep rift between the Revolution and the Catholic Church. It also followed the violent Massacre of the Glacière in October 1791, when revolutionary partisans killed several dozen prisoners in Avignon's papal palace.
key_figures: Pope Pius VI (who protested the annexation)
Jourdan Coupe-Tête (leader of radical faction in Avignon)

sources.name: Decrees of the National Assembly, September 1791 (Archives parlementaires)
Michel Vovelle, The Revolution Against the Church: From Reason to the Supreme Being (1991)

events.certainty_note: Legislative records and local administrative documents survive.

events.title: Tipu Sultan's campaigns and British consolidation in Mysore
events.description: In 1791, the Third Anglo-Mysore War was underway, with British East India Company forces under Lord Cornwallis advancing on Tipu Sultan's capital at Seringapatam. Cornwallis captured the fortified position at Bangalore in March 1791 and laid siege to Seringapatam, though supply problems forced a retreat. Tipu Sultan, simultaneously facing Maratha and Hyderabadi attacks from the north and west as part of a triple alliance against him, was increasingly encircled. The war would end in 1792 with the Treaty of Seringapatam, stripping Tipu of half his territory.
key_figures: Tipu Sultan (ruler of Mysore)
Charles Cornwallis (Governor-General of India and British commander)
Nizam Ali Khan (ruler of Hyderabad, allied with the British)

sources.name: East India Company military dispatches and Cornwallis correspondence (National Archives, UK)
Tipu Sultan's administrative records (partially surviving at the India Office Library)
Kate Brittlebank, Tipu Sultan's Search for Legitimacy (1997)

events.certainty_note: British military records are extensive; Tipu Sultan's own records partially survive.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's campaigns against Gurkhas in Tibet
events.description: In 1791, Gurkha forces from Nepal invaded Tibet for the second time, sacking the great monastery of Tashilhunpo and carrying off enormous wealth. The Qianlong Emperor dispatched a major Qing military expedition under Fuk'anggan to expel the Gurkhas and reassert Chinese suzerainty over Tibet. The Qing forces arrived in Tibet in late 1791 and would pursue the Gurkhas back into Nepal in 1792, ultimately forcing a Nepalese surrender. The campaign led to tightened Qing administrative control over Tibet, including the establishment of the Golden Urn lottery system for selecting reincarnated lamas.
key_figures: Qianlong Emperor (Qing ruler)
Fuk'anggan (Qing general)
Bahadur Shah (regent of Nepal)
Shamarpa Lama (Tibetan religious figure who invited Gurkha intervention)

sources.name: Qing court records and Qianlong Emperor's edicts (Qing Shilu / Veritable Records)
Nepali court chronicles
Peter Schwieger, The Dalai Lama and the Emperor of China (2015)

events.certainty_note: Documented in both Qing imperial records and Nepali sources. The sack of Tashilhunpo is well attested.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Battle of Mačin
events.description: On 9 July 1791 (28 June Old Style), Russian forces under Prince Nikolai Repnin decisively defeated the Ottoman army at the Battle of Mačin (Măcin) on the Danube in what is now Romania. The Ottoman army suffered heavy casualties. This defeat, combined with the fall of the fortress of Anapa to Russian forces in June, compelled the Ottoman Empire to seek peace, leading to the Treaty of Jassy in January 1792. The war confirmed Russian control of the northern Black Sea coast and the annexation of Crimea.
key_figures: Nikolai Repnin (Russian commander)
Selim III (Ottoman Sultan)
Catherine the Great (Russian Empress)

sources.name: Russian military dispatches and Catherine II's correspondence
Ottoman chronicle accounts
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870 (2007)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in both Russian and Ottoman military records.

events.title: Polish Constitution of 3 May (Konstytucja 3 maja)
events.description: On 3 May 1791, the Four-Year Sejm adopted the Constitution of 3 May, Europe's first modern codified national constitution and the world's second after the United States Constitution. It reformed the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth by abolishing the liberum veto (the requirement for unanimous consent that had paralyzed governance), established a constitutional monarchy with separation of powers, extended political rights to townspeople, and placed peasants under government protection. It was a dramatic attempt to save the Commonwealth from the predatory ambitions of Russia, Prussia, and Austria, but it provoked Catherine the Great's hostility and contributed directly to the Second Partition of Poland in 1793.
key_figures: Stanisław August Poniatowski (King of Poland)
Hugo Kołłątaj (co-author)
Ignacy Potocki (co-author)
Catherine the Great (Russian Empress, opposed the constitution)

sources.name: Text of the Constitution of 3 May 1791 (original in the Central Archives of Historical Records, Warsaw)
Proceedings of the Four-Year Sejm (Diariusz Sejmu)
Richard Butterwick, The Polish Revolution and the Catholic Church, 1788–1792 (2012)

events.certainty_note: The constitutional text survives in original form. Parliamentary proceedings are well documented.

events.title: Birmingham Riots (Priestley Riots)
events.description: On 14–17 July 1791, a 'Church and King' mob attacked the homes and laboratories of prominent Dissenters in Birmingham, most notably destroying the house and scientific laboratory of Joseph Priestley, the chemist who had co-discovered oxygen. The rioters were motivated by hostility toward Priestley's support for the French Revolution and religious dissent. Priestley fled to London and eventually emigrated to the United States. The riots reflected the deep polarization in British society over the French Revolution and demonstrated the volatility of radical-conservative tensions.
key_figures: Joseph Priestley (scientist and Dissenting minister)
Edmund Burke (whose Reflections on the Revolution in France had inflamed anti-radical sentiment)

sources.name: Joseph Priestley, An Appeal to the Public on the Subject of the Riots in Birmingham (1791)
Home Office correspondence and trial records (The National Archives, Kew)
Jenny Uglow, The Lunar Men: Five Friends Whose Curiosity Changed the World (2002)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in contemporary accounts, legal records, and Priestley's own writings.
cross_references: 1790-Burke's Reflections on the Revolution in France

events.title: Toussaint Louverture joins the Haitian Revolution
events.description: In the months following the August 1791 slave uprising, Toussaint Louverture — a formerly enslaved man who had been freed for about 15 years and was literate and knowledgeable about military strategy — joined the rebel forces. Initially serving under early leaders like Jean-François and Biassou, Toussaint quickly distinguished himself through military skill and political acumen. He would eventually become the dominant leader of the revolution, transforming an insurgency into a disciplined military and political movement. His entry into the conflict in late 1791 marked the beginning of one of the most remarkable military careers of the era.
key_figures: Toussaint Louverture (revolutionary leader)
Jean-François Papillon (early rebel commander)
Georges Biassou (early rebel commander)

sources.name: Colonial correspondence referencing early rebel leadership (Archives nationales d'outre-mer)
C.L.R. James, The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (1938; revised 1963)
Philippe Girard, Toussaint Louverture: A Revolutionary Life (2016)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Toussaint's entry into the revolt is uncertain; sources differ on whether he joined in September, October, or November 1791. That he joined in late 1791 is the scholarly consensus.

events.title: Vermont admitted as the fourteenth US state
events.description: On 4 March 1791, Vermont was admitted to the Union as the fourteenth state and the first to join after the original thirteen. Vermont had functioned as an independent republic since 1777 and was the first American jurisdiction to partially abolish adult slavery in its constitution. Its admission resolved a long-standing territorial dispute with New York and was paired with Kentucky's admission (1792) to maintain the balance between northern and southern states.
key_figures: Thomas Chittenden (first Governor of Vermont)

sources.name: Act of Congress admitting Vermont, 18 February 1791
Michael Bellesiles, Revolutionary Outlaws: Ethan Allen and the Struggle for Independence on the Early American Frontier (1993)

events.certainty_note: Federal legislative records confirm the date.

events.title: Selim III begins Ottoman military reforms (Nizam-ı Cedid)
events.description: Sultan Selim III, confronting Ottoman military defeats against Russia and Austria, began implementing the Nizam-ı Cedid ('New Order') reforms in 1791–1792. These reforms aimed to create a modern European-style army trained by foreign (primarily French) advisors, alongside fiscal reforms to fund the new military. The Nizam-ı Cedid represented the most ambitious Ottoman modernization effort to date, but faced fierce resistance from the Janissary corps and conservative ulema. The program would ultimately contribute to Selim's deposition and murder in 1807–1808.
key_figures: Selim III (Ottoman Sultan)
Ebubekir Ratip Efendi (Ottoman ambassador whose reports on European armies influenced reform)

sources.name: Ottoman administrative records and Selim III's reform memoranda (hatt-ı hümayun)
Stanford J. Shaw, Between Old and New: The Ottoman Empire Under Sultan Selim III, 1789–1807 (1971)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman archival sources document the reform program, though the exact start date is gradual rather than a single event.

events.title: Luigi Galvani publishes De viribus electricitatis
events.description: In 1791, Italian physician Luigi Galvani published De viribus electricitatis in motu musculari commentarius (Commentary on the Effect of Electricity on Muscular Motion), describing his experiments showing that frog legs twitched when touched with two different metals. Galvani attributed this to 'animal electricity' inherent in living tissue. While his theoretical explanation was contested by Alessandro Volta — who argued the electricity came from the contact between dissimilar metals, leading to the invention of the battery — Galvani's work founded the field of bioelectricity and electrophysiology and profoundly influenced both science and culture (inspiring, among other things, Mary Shelley's Frankenstein).
key_figures: Luigi Galvani (physician and physicist)
Alessandro Volta (physicist, who would dispute Galvani's interpretation)

sources.name: Luigi Galvani, De viribus electricitatis in motu musculari commentarius (1791), published in the proceedings of the Bologna Academy of Sciences
Marco Bresadola, 'Medicine and Science in the Life of Luigi Galvani', Brain Research Bulletin 46 (1998)

events.certainty_note: The original publication survives and is dated precisely.

events.title: French Le Chapelier Law bans trade guilds and workers' associations
events.description: On 14 June 1791, the National Assembly passed the Le Chapelier Law, which banned guilds, trade unions, and all forms of workers' associations, including the right to strike. Framed in the language of individual liberty and free enterprise, the law reflected the Revolution's hostility to corporate intermediary bodies between the citizen and the state. It effectively prohibited collective labor action in France for nearly a century — the law was not fully repealed until 1884. It demonstrated the bourgeois character of the early Revolution's economic liberalism.
key_figures: Isaac René Guy le Chapelier (deputy and sponsor of the law)

sources.name: Text of the Le Chapelier Law (Archives parlementaires, 14 June 1791)
William Sewell, Work and Revolution in France: The Language of Labor from the Old Regime to 1848 (1980)

events.certainty_note: Legislative text and parliamentary debates survive.

events.title: George Vancouver expedition departs for the Pacific Northwest
events.description: On 1 April 1791, Captain George Vancouver departed Falmouth, England, commanding HMS Discovery and HMS Chatham on a major expedition to survey the Pacific Northwest coast of North America. The expedition's goals included receiving the territory at Nootka Sound from Spain under the Nootka Convention, surveying the coastline from California to Alaska to determine whether a Northwest Passage existed, and charting the coast in detail. The voyage lasted until 1795 and produced some of the most detailed maritime surveys of the era, establishing British claims in the region.
key_figures: George Vancouver (Royal Navy captain and expedition leader)

sources.name: George Vancouver, A Voyage of Discovery to the North Pacific Ocean and Round the World (1798)
Admiralty records and ship logs (The National Archives, Kew)

events.certainty_note: Vancouver's own published account and Admiralty records provide precise dates.

events.title: Slave revolt in Dominica (Colihaut uprising)
events.description: In early 1791, enslaved people in Dominica staged an uprising influenced by the revolutionary ferment in neighboring French colonies. The revolt, centered in the Colihaut area, was part of a broader pattern of Caribbean slave resistance emboldened by the ideals of the French Revolution and news of the free people of color's political mobilization in Saint-Domingue. While suppressed by British forces, the uprising was one of several that demonstrated the interconnected nature of revolutionary movements across the Caribbean in this period.

sources.name: Colonial Office correspondence (CO 71, The National Archives, Kew)
David Barry Gaspar and David Patrick Geggus, eds., A Turbulent Time: The French Revolution and the Greater Caribbean (1997)

events.certainty_note: Colonial administrative records document the uprising, though details are sparse compared to larger revolts.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested elements: (1) The Bois Caïman ceremony (1791-005): Historian David Geggus has argued that the ceremony's details — particularly the Vodou ritual elements — may be largely legendary, drawn from Antoine Dalmas's hostile 1814 account rather than contemporary evidence. The ceremony has become a foundational national myth for Haiti, but its specific details (the pig sacrifice, the oath) are not attested in any contemporaneous source. The uprising itself is unquestioned; the ceremony's precise nature is not. (2) Mozart's cause of death (1791-010): Over 100 causes of death have been proposed, ranging from rheumatic fever to mercury poisoning to chronic kidney disease. The persistent legend that Antonio Salieri poisoned Mozart has no credible evidence and was already debunked in the 19th century, though it persists in popular culture via Pushkin and the film Amadeus. (3) The Polish Constitution of 3 May (1791-015): Its adoption was partly achieved by political maneuvering — reformers deliberately chose a date when many conservative opponents were still away from Warsaw after Easter recess. Some historians note this raises questions about its democratic legitimacy, though it was subsequently affirmed by a fuller session. (4) Toussaint Louverture's early role (1791-017): Some scholars have questioned whether Toussaint initially helped protect his former master's property before joining the revolt, complicating the narrative of him as an immediate revolutionary. Philippe Girard's 2016 biography presents evidence that Toussaint may have been more cautious and calculating in his early involvement than the heroic narrative suggests.
historiographic_note: 1791 is exceptionally well documented. The French Revolution generated an enormous volume of printed material — newspapers, pamphlets, legislative records, correspondence — making it one of the most densely sourced periods in history. American constitutional records are similarly abundant. The Haitian Revolution, however, suffers from significant source biases: most contemporary accounts were written by white colonists or French officials hostile to the enslaved population, and the voices of the enslaved themselves survive only through mediated accounts. The Qing and Ottoman sources exist in abundance in their respective archives but are underrepresented in English-language historiography. South Asian sources for the Anglo-Mysore War are heavily tilted toward British East India Company records; Tipu Sultan's own administrative records survive only partially. African and Pacific history for this period remains poorly represented in the available sources, reflecting both archival destruction and the biases of colonial-era scholarship.

graph_edges.note: The Flight to Varennes destroyed public trust in the monarchy and accelerated the move toward republicanism
The August 1791 slave uprising was the opening act of the Haitian Revolution, which culminated in independence in 1804
The Bill of Rights was demanded as a condition for ratification of the 1787 Constitution
The Declaration of Pillnitz inflamed French revolutionary hostility and contributed to the declaration of war in April 1792
The constitution provoked Russian and Prussian hostility, directly leading to military intervention and the second partition
The 1791 campaigns set the stage for Tipu Sultan's defeat and the harsh peace of 1792
The Russian victory at Mačin forced the Ottoman Empire to accept the Treaty of Jassy
The Qing expedition dispatched in 1791 pursued the Gurkhas into Nepal in 1792
Galvani's experiments and Volta's reinterpretation of them led directly to the invention of the voltaic pile
Gouges published her Declaration in direct response to the exclusion of women from the new constitution
The August uprising drew Toussaint Louverture into the revolutionary movement
The Flight to Varennes prompted the republican petition that led to the Champ de Mars massacre
Louis XVI's failed flight alarmed European monarchs and prompted the Declaration of Pillnitz
